How would they do that without efficient public transportation? It would have an extremely negative effect because either they're going to have to figure out how to get a car. They're going to have to figure out a way to walk to where they need to be for work. They're going to have to figure out a way to walk to school because even the school bus system is out of whack in the area. There's going to have to be so much more. It's going to take so long to develop this. If it were to happen immediately, there would be absolutely no hope. It has an extremely negative impact unless the city already has an established and efficient public transportation system.

context_qas.question: Does Environmental Concerns have a positive or negative effect on Support for Upzoning, and how significant is this effect? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?

That's not possible anymore. So I would say that the cause of single-family neighborhoods is a time before everything was as ridiculously priced as it is now. It's not feasible anymore, but it used to be at least somewhat feasible in the past. Now it's basically impossible to be able to do that. So the cause of it is just it's pre-established. It already existed decades and decades ago. I would say that these influences just are. I don't think it's a positive or negative influence on cities. I think that it's nice to have areas like that. context_qas.question: What economic effects, both positive and negative, might result from changing zoning laws to allow more multi-family housing?

I'm trying to think of positives. There are certainly more net negatives.

context_qas.question: How do you think the interests of current residents versus future residents should be balanced when making zoning decisions?
context_qas.answer: I think the interests of current residents are certainly the most important. We cannot predict the future. We are not time travelers. Sure, we have people who are professionals who can look at patterns and think about how it might go, but we don't know that for certain. We should worry about the people who are living here now and their futures, not potential people who might be here someday. Because once we start thinking in hypotheticals, people are going to start thinking in the way that benefits them, and powerful people, in other words, typically richer people, are going to have more of a say in the future of places. So I think that it would not benefit the people who are already living somewhere to think of future residents' best interests. I think that we should absolutely highlight and focus on the struggles of current residents and their interests and what they need, because adding a bunch of extra people to something that's not entirely functioning just is a recipe for disaster.

context_qas.question: What role do you think social equity and access to opportunity play in discussions about zoning and housing policy?
context_qas.answer: Here it certainly gets more complicated in that yes, definitely people who can't afford to live somewhere need a place that's low income to live. I just don't think that that should be upzoning. I think that yes, low income housing should be established for sure. It would be very helpful. However, doing that in places that already have an established pattern, that already have an established presence, is detrimental to those places and to those people. It's not going to work. However, of course social equity plays a big amount and of course people have a right to have a place to live. People have a right to shelter, a right to food, a right to water, a right to the internet, a right to electricity. And upzoning areas that are already existing will not do that for them. I don't think so. It'll make everything harder for everybody. When it comes to social equity, I think it has everything to do with it and I think everybody deserves equal opportunity, which they won't get if we just start adding low income housing to areas that are already pre-existing. Areas need to be dictated for that or given for that. Again, very ideally by local government to see what would work the best. Perhaps in expanding cities, for instance, where they can build new schools or bigger schools or focus on an area that can be improved.

context_qas.question: Would small changes in Higher density housing lead to noticeable changes in Support for Upzoning, or would it take larger shifts? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: This question is a little confusing, so I need to take some time to talk through it and think about it. I think that larger shifts would be very detrimental to the population and the economy and just generally everything here. At least here in my city, I'm considering it from a bigger lens as best I can. I think that smaller changes would probably lead for more support or rather less negativity. I think that if things happened very fast, the negative changes would be very noticeable. Whereas if things happened in very little ways and slowly, perhaps the cities would be able to manage it better and the areas might be able to develop in time. And perhaps people won't realize things are getting worse if they do as quickly. Because people are able to turn a blind eye to things as long as they are doing okay a lot of the time. So it's like people might be blissfully ignorant if it's not affecting them. Whereas if there are these big things, then there will be a lot of people talking about it all at once very vocally and more people will be aware of it. So I think the less people that are aware, the less of an effect it will have generally.

context_qas.question: Does Disruption to community have a positive or negative effect on Support for Upzoning, and how significant is this effect? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: Disruption to community has an extremely negative effect on support for upzoning. And I think it is very, very significant. I can't see anybody wanting things to change in that way. I understand that we do need areas for development, like choose specific areas for lower income housing, for new apartment buildings are affordable. But I can't see anybody wanting things to change like that. I can't see any of the neighborhoods nearby being able to function even remotely with that sort of change. I can't see any of the schools being able to handle it. All I can envision are angry parents with how the schools are going. I can consider angry older residents or distressed older residents with how much everything is changing. I can imagine shops dealing with new competition they haven't dealt with before, ones that are barely staying afloat compared to things like Walmart. I think that disruption is one of the biggest effects and probably the thing people would be the most against or feel the most strong about.

I can't see them being able to function that way. I can't see any of where the businesses they used to work at, I can't see that working. I understand that it's important for people to adapt, but I don't think people should have to adapt that significantly, especially in areas that are already established where people have owned homes for over 40, 50 years even. I don't think it's fair to shift things that significantly in established neighbourhoods, old local businesses that have somehow survived things like COVID and monopolies rising, corporations. It doesn't feel fair and it has a very strong effect on me and the way that I feel about it. I assume it would have a very strong effect on them and a very strong effect on their relatives, even relatives living elsewhere. Houses, even single family houses, are already crowded a lot of the time. It's a big family living in one house and the areas are crowded, the schools are crowded, the workplaces, it's already so hard to find a job. I can't see that many, I can't see it working. I can't see it being remotely beneficial for older people.

context_qas.question: To what extent do you support or oppose upzoning policies that allow for higher density housing in traditionally single-family neighborhoods? Please explain your reasoning.
context_qas.answer: I pretty much oppose that. context_qas.question: What do you think are the most significant impacts, positive or negative, of increasing housing density in residential neighborhoods?
context_qas.answer: It's negative from a mother and family point of view. I'm sure businesses like it. I'm sure property tax collectors like it. But I feel like it's taking away the greenery from the neighborhood that we had and just making it more stressful to go anywhere. It's so busy.

context_qas.question: How do you think upzoning policies might affect housing affordability in urban areas?
context_qas.answer: I don't know. They always say it could reduce costs, but even one-bedroom apartments where I live cost $1,800, which is what my mortgage is, so I don't see just them building upward isn't going to make it cheaper. It'll probably cost just as much, but just triple our traffic.

context_qas.question: What impact do you believe increased housing density might have on neighborhood character and quality of life?
context_qas.answer: Quality of life will go down because everybody is out and I don't live in a walkable city. We all drive So you put a thousand people into an up zoned building and now there's a thousand cars so that just Brings down quality of life Character. Yeah, I mean it's kind of an eyesore, but they could build really nice quality luxury ones, I suppose

context_qas.question: How do you think upzoning might affect transportation systems and traffic congestion in cities?
context_qas.answer: My county and my city don't have any transportation systems. There are no buses, no nothing, so traffic congestion is already pretty bad and you add more of these up zonings, which they're doing, and it's already made a negative impact and how long it takes to get somewhere and we all have to drive.

context_qas.question: What role do you believe local government should play in regulating housing development and density?
context_qas.answer: I believe local and federal should have a say in this. You shouldn't just be allowed to say yes to all of this when everyone who lives here says no and we already have some of the highest property taxes in our state of Georgia. So I feel like our government should listen to us and do their job.

context_qas.question: How might environmental concerns factor into decisions about urban density and zoning?
context_qas.answer: This is where government pretends they care. They're trying to build a gigantic data center in the city next to me, same county, and they had a bajillion people show up and fight this over and over again and it's still being approved. Yeah, environmental concerns definitely factor in, but anyone who puts their hand out to collect money for this deal is going to say yes.

context_qas.question: What economic effects, both positive and negative, might result from changing zoning laws to allow more multi-family housing?
context_qas.answer: For business owners, it's probably good for them because more people in the same small area, the more chances you have of people visiting your restaurant or your store. So from that aspect, it's good for business owners. It's just not good for the people who actually live here, work here, drive here.

context_qas.question: How do you think the interests of current residents versus future residents should be balanced when making zoning decisions?
context_qas.answer: think the interest of current residents should be the heaviest balance. It pisses me off when you have tons of us showing up at these town halls, showing up at these hearings, and we're all together saying no please, and they completely ignore us and pass this crap anyway. Then the stuff gets built, more people come, there's more traffic, but our property taxes have gone up every single year regardless of all this. You're taking in tons more property taxes from all those housing developments you've built. Why is my single-family home taxes going up every year? It's BS.

context_qas.question: What role do you think social equity and access to opportunity play in discussions about zoning and housing policy?
context_qas.answer: I think this is a tool that people who make money off of building this crap pretends that it helps with equity and access. It absolutely does not. They're not building government housing or anything like that. These places are like luxury, luxury apartments. They cost more than my house to live in. So I think it's a load of crap when people pretend that this somehow eases the burden for people who can't find homes. It's not that people can't find homes. It's that people aren't paid enough to afford them anymore and inflation has made it even harder. Pay people more and don't inflate the cost of everything. My house price has doubled in seven years. That's ridiculous.

context_qas.question: Is the effect of Higher density housing on Support for Upzoning immediate, or does it take time to develop? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: I think some effects are immediate traffic, for example. Others will take time to develop. Like, does this potentially bring more crime? Does this potentially bring a de-beautification of the roadsides? All these cars, are they throwing out trash? Did we hire more people to help clean up that trash? That sort of thing. It's like that broken window effect, you know, it could, you know, one broken window, the next house, let theirs go without repair. And the next thing you know, your whole neighborhood looks like that.

context_qas.question: What might lead to increases or decreases in Support for Upzoning, and how significant are these effects? Please also indicate if these influences are positive (increasing) or negative (decreasing).
context_qas.answer: You could increase support for upzoning by actually making these houses super affordable, obviously. You could make the support for upzoning increase exponentially by very much so lowering the property taxes that we are all currently paying in this county. I've mentioned before ours are almost the highest in Georgia, if not the highest, and they go up every year. The influence would be positive if you brought down costs.

context_qas.question: Is the effect of Housing affordability on Support for Upzoning immediate, or does it take time to develop? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: Yeah, if you advertise, hey, you know, one-bedroom apartments cost $1,800, these new up-zoned buildings are going to cost $1,000 a month, you would have a ton of support for that right away from people who are struggling to afford to live, which is probably over half the country. So in that way, it would be a positive effect by decreasing costs. There is no positive effect for costs going up. People are struggling.

context_qas.question: Does Traffic congestion have a positive or negative effect on Support for Upzoning, and how significant is this effect? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: Where I live, traffic congestion was already a big problem. It has become a massive problem over the last seven years. They are building tons of apartment complexes, and they are now starting to upzone, like our Costco is upzoning. The effect on traffic congestion is extremely significant. It definitely decreases support for upzoning, I think, for obvious reasons. One of the main reasons.

context_qas.question: How would you describe the relationship between Local government role and Support for Upzoning? Is it a strong or weak connection? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: The relationship between local government and support for upzoning is direct. I feel like there must be kickbacks or something because our local government completely ignores everyone who lives here, every one of our voices. They completely tune us out and they just approve everything. Tons of gas stations, tons of liquor stores, tons of apartment complexes that literally none of us want. It's a pretty strong connection between local government and upzoning support.

context_qas.question: How confident are you that changes in Public opposition lead to changes in Support for Upzoning? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: Well, the public are the ones who have to live with these upzoning changes. So if you don't have public support for upzoning, you're going to have public opposition. They are inverse of each other. Make it affordable, somehow decrease traffic, and you would increase public support. But that's not what they're doing. It's going to remain negative.

context_qas.question: How would you describe the relationship between Property taxes and Support for Upzoning? Is it a strong or weak connection? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: Strong connection. For sure our property taxes are crazy high. They are building all this crap and our property taxes still go up every year. They haven't lowered them at all even though they're bringing in millions more with all the housing that they've built and filled. It's really not fair and they do nothing about it even though we all do use our voice.

context_qas.question: Would small changes in Town hall participation lead to noticeable changes in Support for Upzoning, or would it take larger shifts? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: It seems to make no difference. We used to have tiny town hall participation, if they even held town halls. Over the last five years, participation in town halls and hearings has exploded because of social media. We organize, we plan, we voice our concerns and we come prepared in absolute opposition to the continuously saying yes to every single apartment complex and up zone. I don't know what it would take. We have voted certain people out and they continue to say yes and put their hand out. I don't know how you stop that. It's a pretty strong effect and problem.

context_qas.question: How would you describe the relationship between Luxury Apartments and Support for Upzoning? Is it a strong or weak connection? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: I think obviously luxury apartments are more desirable to look at and have in your community than cheaper apartments or builder grade everything or government housing. I don't think any of these have a positive effect. None of these things are wanted where I live. So I'm not I'm not really sure how you would improve on that when people don't want this. We prefer single-family homes. I don't know why we can't build normal starter single-family homes like we used to back when boomers were buying homes. Why does everything have to be crammed all together?

context_qas.question: Is the effect of Affordability of Homes on Support for Upzoning immediate, or does it take time to develop? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: I don't think anyone believes this is going to make housing more affordable. This is just a lie they tell us to get this stuff passed and pushed through. The reason I say that is because they have built a crap ton of apartment complexes in my county over the past few years, but home prices and apartment rentals have all gone up and property taxes have continued to go up. So they're saying that it's going to affect the affordability of homes in a positive way is not truthful. No one trusts it and it's not going to build support because we know we're being lied to.

context_qas.question: How would you describe the relationship between Immediate effects and Support for Upzoning? Is it a strong or weak connection? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: Immediate effects, if they're positive, probably increase support for upzoning. But overall, I just think it's a negative. It's not something that the suburbs wanted. We don't live in a giant city. We didn't want our suburbs to become that way. So it's a negative effect overall, regardless of how they spin it.

context_qas.question: How would you describe the relationship between Upzoning support and Support for Upzoning? Is it a strong or weak connection? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: I don't understand that question, the relationship between upzoning support and support for upzoning. I guess they're inverse, right? Because people don't want it.

context_qas.question: What do you think are the main causes of Upzoning policies, and how certain are you about these relationships? Please also indicate if these influences are positive (increasing) or negative (decreasing).
context_qas.answer: I think the main causes are money. They must get a lot of money for the city when they pass this stuff and let these things be built. I don't know what the transparency is on that. I've not looked it up, but I assume there must be a positive for government officials who say yes to this stuff or they wouldn't say yes to everything. Like they live in these communities too. They can drive the streets and see that it's not desirable to have this stuff, so I'm not really sure why all the yes. I'm not sure what is the main cause if not money or gain in some way.

context_qas.question: How confident are you that changes in Negative effect lead to changes in Support for Upzoning? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: Negative effects absolutely lead to less support for upzoning. I'm very confident in that. You know, more traffic, that's a negative. Lessened support. More trash on the roads, potentially more crime. Schools are now too full and so your kid has to go to class in an un-air-conditioned trailer because their school can't hold the number of new, the number of students who've come since we've built all this. Negative effects definitely decrease support. Positive effects would increase support.

context_qas.question: Does Population density have a positive or negative effect on Support for Upzoning, and how significant is this effect? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: Population density is definitely an issue. I don't see how packing even more people into a smaller area with up zoning is going to help that problem. So I don't really understand why they word it that way. It's not, you know, these people don't live here now. You're building all this stuff to bring them here. I think there's other ways that we could deal with population density and the lack of affordable housing. There's other ways to do this besides this model.

source_qa.answer: Population density is definitely going to have a negative effect on support. You build 10 apartment complexes, and bam, that's all in a three-mile area. Now guess what? People are living there and driving from there.

reasoning: The person believes that increased population density from new housing developments immediately and negatively affects support for upzoning.

demographics.occupation: Management, business, science, and arts occupations
demographics.financial_situation: I get by, but money is tight
demographics.neighborhood_safety: Very safe – I rarely worry about crime
demographics.health_insurance: no disability, with private health insurance

context_qas.question: To what extent do you support or oppose upzoning policies that allow for higher density housing in traditionally single-family neighborhoods? Please explain your reasoning.
context_qas.answer: I pretty much oppose that. The neighborhood that I live in is currently doing this, and we already have schools, it's completely full, car lines are out of control, traffic is terrible, and I understand the reasoning behind it, but it feels wrong to put in single family neighborhoods.

context_qas.question: What do you think are the most significant impacts, positive or negative, of increasing housing density in residential neighborhoods?
context_qas.answer: It's negative from a mother and family point of view. I'm sure businesses like it. I'm sure property tax collectors like it. But I feel like it's taking away the greenery from the neighborhood that we had and just making it more stressful to go anywhere. It's so busy.

context_qas.question: How do you think upzoning policies might affect housing affordability in urban areas?
context_qas.answer: I don't know. They always say it could reduce costs, but even one-bedroom apartments where I live cost $1,800, which is what my mortgage is, so I don't see just them building upward isn't going to make it cheaper. It'll probably cost just as much, but just triple our traffic.

context_qas.question: What impact do you believe increased housing density might have on neighborhood character and quality of life?
context_qas.answer: Quality of life will go down because everybody is out and I don't live in a walkable city. We all drive So you put a thousand people into an up zoned building and now there's a thousand cars so that just Brings down quality of life Character. Yeah, I mean it's kind of an eyesore, but they could build really nice quality luxury ones, I suppose

context_qas.question: How do you think upzoning might affect transportation systems and traffic congestion in cities?
context_qas.answer: My county and my city don't have any transportation systems. There are no buses, no nothing, so traffic congestion is already pretty bad and you add more of these up zonings, which they're doing, and it's already made a negative impact and how long it takes to get somewhere and we all have to drive.

context_qas.question: What role do you believe local government should play in regulating housing development and density?
context_qas.answer: I believe local and federal should have a say in this. You shouldn't just be allowed to say yes to all of this when everyone who lives here says no and we already have some of the highest property taxes in our state of Georgia. So I feel like our government should listen to us and do their job.

context_qas.question: How might environmental concerns factor into decisions about urban density and zoning?
context_qas.answer: This is where government pretends they care. They're trying to build a gigantic data center in the city next to me, same county, and they had a bajillion people show up and fight this over and over again and it's still being approved. Yeah, environmental concerns definitely factor in, but anyone who puts their hand out to collect money for this deal is going to say yes.

context_qas.question: What economic effects, both positive and negative, might result from changing zoning laws to allow more multi-family housing?
context_qas.answer: For business owners, it's probably good for them because more people in the same small area, the more chances you have of people visiting your restaurant or your store. So from that aspect, it's good for business owners. It's just not good for the people who actually live here, work here, drive here.

context_qas.question: How do you think the interests of current residents versus future residents should be balanced when making zoning decisions?
context_qas.answer: think the interest of current residents should be the heaviest balance. It pisses me off when you have tons of us showing up at these town halls, showing up at these hearings, and we're all together saying no please, and they completely ignore us and pass this crap anyway. Then the stuff gets built, more people come, there's more traffic, but our property taxes have gone up every single year regardless of all this. You're taking in tons more property taxes from all those housing developments you've built. Why is my single-family home taxes going up every year? It's BS.

context_qas.question: What role do you think social equity and access to opportunity play in discussions about zoning and housing policy?
context_qas.answer: I think this is a tool that people who make money off of building this crap pretends that it helps with equity and access. It absolutely does not. They're not building government housing or anything like that. These places are like luxury, luxury apartments. They cost more than my house to live in. So I think it's a load of crap when people pretend that this somehow eases the burden for people who can't find homes. It's not that people can't find homes. It's that people aren't paid enough to afford them anymore and inflation has made it even harder. Pay people more and don't inflate the cost of everything. My house price has doubled in seven years. That's ridiculous.

context_qas.question: Is the effect of Higher density housing on Support for Upzoning immediate, or does it take time to develop? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: I think some effects are immediate traffic, for example. Others will take time to develop. Like, does this potentially bring more crime? Does this potentially bring a de-beautification of the roadsides? All these cars, are they throwing out trash? Did we hire more people to help clean up that trash? That sort of thing. It's like that broken window effect, you know, it could, you know, one broken window, the next house, let theirs go without repair. And the next thing you know, your whole neighborhood looks like that.

context_qas.question: What might lead to increases or decreases in Support for Upzoning, and how significant are these effects? Please also indicate if these influences are positive (increasing) or negative (decreasing).
context_qas.answer: You could increase support for upzoning by actually making these houses super affordable, obviously. You could make the support for upzoning increase exponentially by very much so lowering the property taxes that we are all currently paying in this county. I've mentioned before ours are almost the highest in Georgia, if not the highest, and they go up every year. The influence would be positive if you brought down costs.

context_qas.question: Is the effect of Housing affordability on Support for Upzoning immediate, or does it take time to develop? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: Yeah, if you advertise, hey, you know, one-bedroom apartments cost $1,800, these new up-zoned buildings are going to cost $1,000 a month, you would have a ton of support for that right away from people who are struggling to afford to live, which is probably over half the country. So in that way, it would be a positive effect by decreasing costs. There is no positive effect for costs going up. People are struggling.

context_qas.question: Does Traffic congestion have a positive or negative effect on Support for Upzoning, and how significant is this effect? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: Where I live, traffic congestion was already a big problem. It has become a massive problem over the last seven years. They are building tons of apartment complexes, and they are now starting to upzone, like our Costco is upzoning. The effect on traffic congestion is extremely significant. It definitely decreases support for upzoning, I think, for obvious reasons. One of the main reasons.

context_qas.question: How would you describe the relationship between Local government role and Support for Upzoning? Is it a strong or weak connection? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: The relationship between local government and support for upzoning is direct. I feel like there must be kickbacks or something because our local government completely ignores everyone who lives here, every one of our voices. They completely tune us out and they just approve everything. Tons of gas stations, tons of liquor stores, tons of apartment complexes that literally none of us want. It's a pretty strong connection between local government and upzoning support.

context_qas.question: How confident are you that changes in Public opposition lead to changes in Support for Upzoning? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: Well, the public are the ones who have to live with these upzoning changes. So if you don't have public support for upzoning, you're going to have public opposition. They are inverse of each other. Make it affordable, somehow decrease traffic, and you would increase public support. But that's not what they're doing. It's going to remain negative.

context_qas.question: Is the effect of Increased population density on Support for Upzoning immediate, or does it take time to develop? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: I think the effect is pretty immediate. You know, you build 10 apartment complexes, each one holds 200 apartments, and bam, that's all in a three-mile area. Now guess what? People are living there and driving from there. They're all on the roads with us and they weren't here a year ago. That's definitely going to have a negative effect on support. Population density is an issue.

context_qas.question: How would you describe the relationship between Property taxes and Support for Upzoning? Is it a strong or weak connection? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: Strong connection. For sure our property taxes are crazy high. They are building all this crap and our property taxes still go up every year. They haven't lowered them at all even though they're bringing in millions more with all the housing that they've built and filled. It's really not fair and they do nothing about it even though we all do use our voice.

context_qas.question: How would you describe the relationship between Luxury Apartments and Support for Upzoning? Is it a strong or weak connection? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: I think obviously luxury apartments are more desirable to look at and have in your community than cheaper apartments or builder grade everything or government housing. I don't think any of these have a positive effect. None of these things are wanted where I live. So I'm not I'm not really sure how you would improve on that when people don't want this. We prefer single-family homes. I don't know why we can't build normal starter single-family homes like we used to back when boomers were buying homes. Why does everything have to be crammed all together?

context_qas.question: Is the effect of Affordability of Homes on Support for Upzoning immediate, or does it take time to develop? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: I don't think anyone believes this is going to make housing more affordable. This is just a lie they tell us to get this stuff passed and pushed through. The reason I say that is because they have built a crap ton of apartment complexes in my county over the past few years, but home prices and apartment rentals have all gone up and property taxes have continued to go up. So they're saying that it's going to affect the affordability of homes in a positive way is not truthful. No one trusts it and it's not going to build support because we know we're being lied to.

context_qas.question: How would you describe the relationship between Immediate effects and Support for Upzoning? Is it a strong or weak connection? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: Immediate effects, if they're positive, probably increase support for upzoning. But overall, I just think it's a negative. It's not something that the suburbs wanted. We don't live in a giant city. We didn't want our suburbs to become that way. So it's a negative effect overall, regardless of how they spin it.

context_qas.question: How would you describe the relationship between Upzoning support and Support for Upzoning? Is it a strong or weak connection? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: I don't understand that question, the relationship between upzoning support and support for upzoning. I guess they're inverse, right? Because people don't want it.

context_qas.question: What do you think are the main causes of Upzoning policies, and how certain are you about these relationships? Please also indicate if these influences are positive (increasing) or negative (decreasing).
context_qas.answer: I think the main causes are money. They must get a lot of money for the city when they pass this stuff and let these things be built. I don't know what the transparency is on that. I've not looked it up, but I assume there must be a positive for government officials who say yes to this stuff or they wouldn't say yes to everything. Like they live in these communities too. They can drive the streets and see that it's not desirable to have this stuff, so I'm not really sure why all the yes. I'm not sure what is the main cause if not money or gain in some way.

context_qas.question: How confident are you that changes in Negative effect lead to changes in Support for Upzoning? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: Negative effects absolutely lead to less support for upzoning. I'm very confident in that. You know, more traffic, that's a negative. Lessened support. More trash on the roads, potentially more crime. Schools are now too full and so your kid has to go to class in an un-air-conditioned trailer because their school can't hold the number of new, the number of students who've come since we've built all this. Negative effects definitely decrease support. Positive effects would increase support.

context_qas.question: Does Population density have a positive or negative effect on Support for Upzoning, and how significant is this effect? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: Population density is definitely an issue. I don't see how packing even more people into a smaller area with up zoning is going to help that problem. So I don't really understand why they word it that way. It's not, you know, these people don't live here now. You're building all this stuff to bring them here. I think there's other ways that we could deal with population density and the lack of affordable housing. There's other ways to do this besides this model.

source_qa.answer: It seems to make no difference. We organize, we plan, we voice our concerns... but they continue to say yes.

context_qas.answer: I believe that higher tax revenue would have a positive effect for support for upzoning from politicians and citizens at large, however, for current residents in the neighborhood, I do not believe that higher tax revenue will have any effect at all on their negative support for upzoning

context_qas.question: To what extent do you support or oppose upzoning policies that allow for higher density housing in traditionally single-family neighborhoods? Please explain your reasoning.
context_qas.answer: I don't feel like they should have higher density housing mixed in with traditional single family neighborhoods. I don't see how mixing the two is good when people are paying a lot of money to live in their own separate houses.

context_qas.question: What do you think are the most significant impacts, positive or negative, of increasing housing density in residential neighborhoods?
context_qas.answer: The most significant impact would be negative and that would be too many people in a small area. It causes traffic jams, it causes people to be late for work, it causes more car accidents, more crime. You just can't put a lot of people into a small area.

context_qas.question: How do you think upzoning policies might affect housing affordability in urban areas?
context_qas.answer: It would probably eventually make housing more affordable, but in the area we're in right now, they're still charging ridiculous amounts to pack people in because of greed.

context_qas.question: What impact do you believe increased housing density might have on neighborhood character and quality of life?
context_qas.answer: I think the quality of life would go down because nobody's happy being stuffed like sardines into neighborhoods. People are happier when they have places to exercise and stretch their legs and have privacy. I think the character of the neighborhoods might look trashy due to they're so full of people.

context_qas.question: How do you think upzoning might affect transportation systems and traffic congestion in cities?
context_qas.answer: If the cities don't plan ahead and there's not enough ways for people to get in and out of the city, like say only one highway, then there's going to be congestion and transportation is always behind so they won't have anything for the people to use, such as buses or trains. If they could plan that ahead of time, it wouldn't cause a problem.

context_qas.question: What role do you believe local government should play in regulating housing development and density?
context_qas.answer: Local government should definitely step in and make sure that housing development is done properly and safely and up to code and they should also look at the infrastructure of the area to see if it can hold that many people and stop it until it can.

demographics.housing_experience: For the past five years, I’ve been renting the same single family home here in Lewistown, PA. I was in section 8 housing three years ago. That was a different experience because my rent was a lot lower, I received food stamps, which made me eligible to get section 8 housing help where they would pay some of my rent for me each month. However, a few years ago that all changed and we no longer qualify for section 8 housing help. due to this we had to move because we couldn’t afford where we were living at anymore because we didn’t have the help anymore. That’s when we moved where we are currently living now. I needed a place that I could afford on my own without all the extra help. My landlord is amazing compared to my last landlord. She is great. One thing that I have definitely noticed Is how much my electricity bill keeps going up like each year literally, my last electric bill was over $1000 because we’re still paying off past due bills. I currently do get lie heap, which is where the government helps me pay my electric bill once a year and they only pay about. I think it was $300 this year. I only get that help one time During one month in the year. honestly, that electric bill is what makes it tough to be able to afford anything else including the price of groceries feels like the economy and all the things that go with it like our gas and our groceries and our healthcare medication’s all just keeps going up in price.

context_qas.question: How do you think upzoning might affect transportation systems and traffic congestion in cities?
context_qas.answer: in a bigger city I can only imagine it could be a lot worse if they added a bunch more people without also improving the roads or public transportation. More people means more cars that’s just fact. So unless they really invest in things like buses or trains at the same time it’s seems like up zoning could definitely make traffic a bigger problem in areas like the city!

context_qas.question: What role do you believe local government should play in regulating housing development and density?
context_qas.answer: I think the local government plays a big role! mainly because they need to think long-term, just like all communities and nations and people do, not just what’s happening right now. they should definitely listen to people and what they want for the town. Setting some rules bout how dense things can get, where new buildings can go, making sure there’s enough parking spaces, also green space is very important too in my opinion. It’s about finding a balance that literally works for everyone equally and fair!

context_qas.question: How might environmental concerns factor into decisions about urban density and zoning?
context_qas.answer: well, for example, where I live in PA we care about keeping things clean and green. in larger and bigger cities it’s probably even more important. I feel like they need to consider things like green spaces and maybe parks or just more trees along the streets. I think how the building is designed is important too. Things like using energy and making sure they’re not blocking sunlight for other buildings. Also have to thing about managing waste and water PROPERLY! So it doesn’t harm our environment.

context_qas.question: What economic effects, both positive and negative, might result from changing zoning laws to allow more multi-family housing?
context_qas.answer: Some positives would be more people living closer to downtown could mean more customers for our local shops and restaurants. Maybe some new buisnesses would open up to! Or could also create more construction jobs. On the downside it could change the character of the neighborhoods too much meaning that it might make some people who currently live here or there want to move out which could be bad for the local economy! property values could also possibly change which could ultimately affect property taxes. Feels like it’s a bit like gambling.

context_qas.question: How do you think the interests of current residents versus future residents should be balanced when making zoning decisions?
context_qas.answer: You have people who have lived here their entire lives. They like things the way they are and their opinions matter. You also want it to be a place where younger people can afford to live and raise a family so you have to think about the future. our local government really needs to listen to every one every local resident every citizen, etc.. get their feedback!

context_qas.question: What role do you think social equity and access to opportunity play in discussions about zoning and housing policy?
context_qas.answer: if we only allow single-family homes in certain areas it can keep housing prices high and make it harder for people with lower incomes to live in those neighborhoods. Which might have better schools and education and better job opportunities. Thinking about social equity means making sure everyone has a fair shot at finding a decent place to live and access what the town has to offer. Zoning policies should try to create more diverse housing options so it’s not just for one group of people.

context_qas.question: What role do you believe local government should play in regulating housing development and density?
context_qas.answer: I think the local government plays a big role! mainly because they need to think long-term, just like all communities and nations and people do, not just what’s happening right now. they should definitely listen to people and what they want for the town. Setting some rules bout how dense things can get, where new buildings can go, making sure there’s enough parking spaces, also green space is very important too in my opinion. It’s about finding a balance that literally works for everyone equally and fair!

context_qas.question: How might environmental concerns factor into decisions about urban density and zoning?
context_qas.answer: well, for example, where I live in PA we care about keeping things clean and green. in larger and bigger cities it’s probably even more important. I feel like they need to consider things like green spaces and maybe parks or just more trees along the streets. I think how the building is designed is important too. Things like using energy and making sure they’re not blocking sunlight for other buildings. Also have to thing about managing waste and water PROPERLY! So it doesn’t harm our environment.

context_qas.question: What economic effects, both positive and negative, might result from changing zoning laws to allow more multi-family housing?
context_qas.answer: Some positives would be more people living closer to downtown could mean more customers for our local shops and restaurants. Maybe some new buisnesses would open up to! Or could also create more construction jobs. On the downside it could change the character of the neighborhoods too much meaning that it might make some people who currently live here or there want to move out which could be bad for the local economy! property values could also possibly change which could ultimately affect property taxes. Feels like it’s a bit like gambling.

context_qas.question: How do you think the interests of current residents versus future residents should be balanced when making zoning decisions?
context_qas.answer: You have people who have lived here their entire lives. They like things the way they are and their opinions matter. You also want it to be a place where younger people can afford to live and raise a family so you have to think about the future. our local government really needs to listen to every one every local resident every citizen, etc.. get their feedback!

context_qas.question: What role do you think social equity and access to opportunity play in discussions about zoning and housing policy?
context_qas.answer: if we only allow single-family homes in certain areas it can keep housing prices high and make it harder for people with lower incomes to live in those neighborhoods. Which might have better schools and education and better job opportunities. Thinking about social equity means making sure everyone has a fair shot at finding a decent place to live and access what the town has to offer. Zoning policies should try to create more diverse housing options so it’s not just for one group of people.

context_qas.question: Does Potential benefits of higher density housing have a positive or negative effect on Support for Upzoning, and how significant is this effect? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: If I saw real benefits to higher density like more local businesses or lower housing costs eventually that would make me more open to upzoning so it would have a positive impact and increases my support! I would say that it’s definitely significant if I could see the upsides for the town and its residents I would be much more willing to get past some of the concerns about change. if the benefits seemed small or uncertain then I would probably be less supportive.

context_qas.question: Does Housing Prices have a positive or negative effect on Support for Upzoning, and how significant is this effect? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?

A few things that could change my opinion towards zoning would be the potential for increased local businesses Worries about traffic nd parking seeing more cars and less parking around town is a negative influence. 
affordability of housing. if up zoning seemed like could make house more affordable for young people or those struggling that would definitely be a positive and a strong influence. Also, if the local government had a clear plan and involve the community that would be extremely positive!

context_qas.question: What factors affect Positive impact on local businesses, and which ones have the strongest influence? Please also indicate if these influences are positive (increasing) or negative (decreasing).
context_qas.answer: if housing prices got high the idea that up zoning could lower them would positively affect my opinion and support but if prices were okay or if i was worried about my property value it could negatively affect my support. it honestly depends on the situation.

context_qas.question: What factors do you think influence Support for Upzoning, and how strong is their impact? Please also indicate if these influences are positive (increasing) or negative (decreasing).

context_qas.question: Is the effect of Increased traffic on Support for Upzoning immediate, or does it take time to develop? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?

context_qas.question: How confident are you that changes in More housing options lead to changes in Support for Upzoning? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?

context_qas.question: Is the effect of Neighborhood character on Support for Upzoning immediate, or does it take time to develop? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: It would have a negative effect on my support. context_qas.question: Would small changes in Traffic Congestion lead to noticeable changes in Support for Upzoning, or would it take larger shifts? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?

context_qas.question: Does Increased Population have a positive or negative effect on Support for Upzoning, and how significant is this effect? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: Increased population would have a negative effect on my support. some growth is OK, a notable increase could strain our local resources like schools and roads and it might change the small town feeling I appreciate. context_qas.question: How would you describe the relationship between Balanced fairness and Support for Upzoning? Is it a strong or weak connection? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: The relationship between balanced fairness and support for up zoning would be positive and strong! And on the other hand if I thought the potential outcomes were unfair my support would decrease. Fairness is a big deal to me personally!

context_qas.question: Would small changes in Environmental cleanliness lead to noticeable changes in Support for Upzoning, or would it take larger shifts? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: Even small changes in environmental cleanliness in Lewistown would likely lead to notable negative changes in my support. The effect would be strong but on the other hand small positive changes in environmental cleanliness that were clearly linked to a thoughtful up zoning plan like more green spaces or better infrastructure could lead to a positive in my support.

context_qas.question: How confident are you that changes in Support for local businesses lead to changes in Support for Upzoning? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?

If it was more obvious that more residents living closer to downtown were spending money locally the effect would definitely be stronger.A boost to our local businesses is a big plus for me personally.

context_qas.question: Does Local government listening have a positive or negative effect on Support for Upzoning, and how significant is this effect? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: local government listening would have a strong positive effect on my support. context_qas.question: How would you describe the relationship between Social Equity and Support for Upzoning? Is it a strong or weak connection? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: Social equity has a strong positive connection to my support for upzoning. if it means a fairer town with more opportunities for everyone.  IM ALL FOR IT. if it makes things less fair. Then I am ALL AGAINST IT!

context_qas.question: Does Access to Opportunity have a positive or negative effect on Support for Upzoning, and how significant is this effect? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: access to opportunities has a strong positive effect on my support for up zoning. demographics.housing_experience: For the past five years, I’ve been renting the same single family home here in Lewistown, PA. I was in section 8 housing three years ago. That was a different experience because my rent was a lot lower, I received food stamps, which made me eligible to get section 8 housing help where they would pay some of my rent for me each month. However, a few years ago that all changed and we no longer qualify for section 8 housing help. due to this we had to move because we couldn’t afford where we were living at anymore because we didn’t have the help anymore. That’s when we moved where we are currently living now. I needed a place that I could afford on my own without all the extra help. My landlord is amazing compared to my last landlord. She is great. One thing that I have definitely noticed Is how much my electricity bill keeps going up like each year literally, my last electric bill was over $1000 because we’re still paying off past due bills. I currently do get lie heap, which is where the government helps me pay my electric bill once a year and they only pay about. I think it was $300 this year. I only get that help one time During one month in the year. honestly, that electric bill is what makes it tough to be able to afford anything else including the price of groceries feels like the economy and all the things that go with it like our gas and our groceries and our healthcare medication’s all just keeps going up in price.

I feel like they need to consider things like green spaces and maybe parks or just more trees along the streets. I think how the building is designed is important too. Things like using energy and making sure they’re not blocking sunlight for other buildings. Also have to thing about managing waste and water PROPERLY! So it doesn’t harm our environment.

context_qas.question: What economic effects, both positive and negative, might result from changing zoning laws to allow more multi-family housing?
context_qas.answer: Some positives would be more people living closer to downtown could mean more customers for our local shops and restaurants. Maybe some new buisnesses would open up to! Or could also create more construction jobs. On the downside it could change the character of the neighborhoods too much meaning that it might make some people who currently live here or there want to move out which could be bad for the local economy! property values could also possibly change which could ultimately affect property taxes. Feels like it’s a bit like gambling.

context_qas.question: How do you think the interests of current residents versus future residents should be balanced when making zoning decisions?
context_qas.answer: You have people who have lived here their entire lives. They like things the way they are and their opinions matter. You also want it to be a place where younger people can afford to live and raise a family so you have to think about the future. our local government really needs to listen to every one every local resident every citizen, etc.. get their feedback!

context_qas.question: What role do you think social equity and access to opportunity play in discussions about zoning and housing policy?
context_qas.answer: if we only allow single-family homes in certain areas it can keep housing prices high and make it harder for people with lower incomes to live in those neighborhoods. Which might have better schools and education and better job opportunities. Thinking about social equity means making sure everyone has a fair shot at finding a decent place to live and access what the town has to offer. Zoning policies should try to create more diverse housing options so it’s not just for one group of people.

context_qas.question: Does Potential benefits of higher density housing have a positive or negative effect on Support for Upzoning, and how significant is this effect? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: If I saw real benefits to higher density like more local businesses or lower housing costs eventually that would make me more open to upzoning so it would have a positive impact and increases my support! I would say that it’s definitely significant if I could see the upsides for the town and its residents I would be much more willing to get past some of the concerns about change. if the benefits seemed small or uncertain then I would probably be less supportive.

context_qas.question: Does Housing Prices have a positive or negative effect on Support for Upzoning, and how significant is this effect? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer:
A few things that could change my opinion towards zoning would be the potential for increased local businesses Worries about traffic nd parking seeing more cars and less parking around town is a negative influence. 
affordability of housing. if up zoning seemed like could make house more affordable for young people or those struggling that would definitely be a positive and a strong influence. Also, if the local government had a clear plan and involve the community that would be extremely positive!

context_qas.question: What factors affect Positive impact on local businesses, and which ones have the strongest influence? Please also indicate if these influences are positive (increasing) or negative (decreasing).
context_qas.answer: if housing prices got high the idea that up zoning could lower them would positively affect my opinion and support but if prices were okay or if i was worried about my property value it could negatively affect my support. it honestly depends on the situation.

context_qas.question: What factors do you think influence Support for Upzoning, and how strong is their impact? Please also indicate if these influences are positive (increasing) or negative (decreasing).
context_qas.answer: My support for up zoning depends on more local businesses - positive-strong. Traffic/parking worries - negative. Potential for more affordable housing - positive. How well it’s planned with community input - positive.

context_qas.answer: The worry about increased traffic on my support for up zoning would be pretty immediate and negative. Even the thought of more conjestion on our local roads especially during rush-hour or around school times makes me less supportive right away. Traffic flow impacts daily life here.

context_qas.question: How confident are you that changes in More housing options lead to changes in Support for Upzoning? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?

context_qas.question: Is the effect of Neighborhood character on Support for Upzoning immediate, or does it take time to develop? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: It would have a negative effect on my support. It’s strong because it’s about the place. I call home.

context_qas.question: Would small changes in Traffic Congestion lead to noticeable changes in Support for Upzoning, or would it take larger shifts? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: It would likely lead to noticeable negative changes in my support for zoning, the effect would probably be strong. enough to make me think twice about supporting more density . it wouldn’t take a huge increase to make me feel less favorable towards it.

context_qas.question: Does Increased Population have a positive or negative effect on Support for Upzoning, and how significant is this effect? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: Increased population would have a negative effect on my support. some growth is OK, a notable increase could strain our local resources like schools and roads and it might change the small town feeling I appreciate. The strength of this negative effect would probably be strong or somewhat strong. Depending on how significant the population increase was projected.

context_qas.question: How would you describe the relationship between Balanced fairness and Support for Upzoning? Is it a strong or weak connection? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: The relationship between balanced fairness and support for up zoning would be positive and strong! If I believed that the zoning plan was well thought out and planned and aimed for balanced outcomes that were fair to everyone involved my support would definitely increase a lot. And on the other hand if I thought the potential outcomes were unfair my support would decrease. Fairness is a big deal to me personally!

context_qas.question: Would small changes in Environmental cleanliness lead to noticeable changes in Support for Upzoning, or would it take larger shifts? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: Even small changes in environmental cleanliness in Lewistown would likely lead to notable negative changes in my support. We value the natural beauty around here and even a slight increase in littering and litter in general, pollution or strain on resources due to increased density would be very concerning. The effect would be strong but on the other hand small positive changes in environmental cleanliness that were clearly linked to a thoughtful up zoning plan like more green spaces or better infrastructure could lead to a positive in my support.

context_qas.answer: I am strongly confident that changes in my support for local businesses would lead to changes in my support. it has a positive effect. if I saw clear evidence that upzoning was helping our local shops and restaurants thrive the en maybe more foot traffic and. new businesses opening then my support would definitely increase. If it was more obvious that more residents living closer to downtown were spending money locally the effect would definitely be stronger.A boost to our local businesses is a big plus for me personally.

context_qas.question: Does Local government listening have a positive or negative effect on Support for Upzoning, and how significant is this effect? Does it have a positive effect (increasing it) or a negative effect (decreasing it)? How strong is this effect?
context_qas.answer: local government listening would have a strong positive effect on my support. If I felt like our local government was truly listening and hearing our concerns and ideas of the community and holding discussions and debates and taking feedback seriously along with being transparent about their plans I would definitely be much more supportive to changes even if I had some reservations about to. It builds trust and makes me feel like the outcome will be better for everyone. If I felt like decisions were being made without our input that would create strong negative feelings and decrease my support.
